The author of Hebrews calls you to fix your eyes on Jesus and run your race with endurance—but He does not promise you a comfortable path. Instead, He tells you to expect discipline. This is not punishment for your sins; Christ absorbed that on the cross. Rather, the hardships you face, the struggles that test your resolve, the disappointments that make you question whether God still cares—these are His way of training you as a beloved son or daughter. The Greek word for discipline here means "training" or "instruction through correction," the same way a father shapes his child. To receive discipline is to receive proof that you belong to God. Without it, you would be illegitimate, fatherless. The weight of this truth should steady you: your suffering is not evidence that God has abandoned you. It is evidence that He claims you as His own and cares too much about your character to leave you unchanged.

But do not mistake endurance for passive resignation. The author demands that you examine your hearts for the roots of bitterness, for sexual immorality, for the kind of godlessness that treats eternal things as worthless. He holds up Esau as a warning—a man who sold his birthright for temporary satisfaction and later wept for what he had lost, unable to find repentance. His tears came too late. The issue is not that God refused to forgive him; it is that his own choices had already hardened his heart so thoroughly that he could no longer genuinely turn back. You stand in a different position. You have come not to Mount Sinai with its terrifying fire and thunder, but to Mount Zion, to Jesus the mediator of a new covenant whose sprinkled blood speaks better than Abel's blood crying out for justice. This is incomprehensible grace. Do not treat it lightly.

What Christ offers you is an unshakable kingdom while all earthly things—comfort, reputation, security, even life itself—are being shaken and removed. This is why gratitude is not optional; it is the only reasonable response. You are being trained for eternity. Every hardship that drives you deeper into dependence on God, every moment when you must choose obedience over comfort, every tear shed in genuine repentance—these are not distractions from your faith but the very substance of it. The author closes with a single, searing phrase: "Our God is a consuming fire." He does not present this as a threat meant to terrify but as the ultimate truth that should reshape how you worship. A consuming fire purifies. It destroys what is false. It burns away everything that does not belong. If you have truly come to Jesus, then you have come to be made holy, and holiness is not negotiable.
